Proteins in the plasma membrane are divided into two categories – *integral proteins* or *peripheral proteins*. Most integral proteins are also referred to as *transmembrane proteins*, meaning they span right across the lipid bilayer and are in contact with both the intracellular and extracellular fluid. In contrast, peripheral proteins tend to associate with membrane lipids or integral proteins at either the inner or outer surface of the membrane. Membrane proteins have a variety of functions which are essential for maintaining cell viability.

![A basic structure of the plasma membrane diagram parts are labeled as integral (transmembrane) proteins, peripheral proteins, cholesterol, pore, channel protein, and peripheral protein, Phospholipids are polar heads, fatty acid tails, and polar heads.](Attachments/Books/biochemistry-for-sport-and-exercise-maclaren/c03f012.png)

## Definition

Membrane proteins are proteins associated with the plasma membrane, classified into two categories: *integral proteins* and *peripheral proteins*. Integral proteins are typically *transmembrane proteins* that span across the lipid bilayer, contacting both intracellular and extracellular fluid. Peripheral proteins associate with membrane lipids or integral proteins at either the inner or outer surface of the membrane.

## Core Principles

- **Integral (transmembrane) proteins** span the entire lipid bilayer, exposing domains to both the intracellular and extracellular fluid.
- **Peripheral proteins** do not penetrate the bilayer; instead they bind to membrane lipids or to integral proteins, attaching to either the inner or outer membrane surface.
- Membrane proteins collectively perform a variety of functions essential for maintaining cell viability.

## Sub-concepts

- Integral proteins / Transmembrane proteins
- Peripheral proteins
- Channel proteins and pores (as depicted alongside integral and peripheral proteins in the plasma membrane structure)

## Practical Applications

Membrane proteins underpin cell viability through their diverse functional roles (e.g., forming channels and pores for transport across the bilayer), making them fundamental to physiological processes in all cells.

- Cholesterol
- Channel proteins / pores
